common_name: Husky Red Tomato (Compact Semi-Determinate Hybrid Tomato for Containers and Small Gardens)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; Husky Red is a semi-determinate tomato with a compact growth habit that reaches 4–5 feet and produces a heavy concentrated set of medium-large red slicing tomatoes before tapering off, making it more manageable in containers and small raised beds than fully indeterminate varieties. In zone 9, transplant March–April for summer production. Husky Red carries VFNt disease resistance (Verticillium, Fusarium, nematodes, tobacco mosaic virus) — a practical package for zone 9's disease pressures. The semi-determinate habit (more compact than indeterminate varieties, more productive than determinate types) allows the plant to be managed with minimal staking — a light cage or 4-foot stake is sufficient compared to the 6–7 foot support needed for indeterminate types. Fruits average 8–10 oz, round, and ripen to deep red with good flavor.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 70–85°F; transplant after last frost and stake lightly. Harvest when uniformly deep red and gives slightly to pressure. Husky Red is a hybrid tomato variety bred for compact growing habit, good disease resistance, and productive fruit set in a semi-determinate plant that provides a midpoint between the sprawling unpredictability of indeterminate varieties and the one-time heavy crop of fully determinate types. The 'Husky' designation refers to the sturdy, self-supporting nature of the plant — Husky varieties were developed specifically to stand upright with minimal staking, making them practical for gardeners without room for large cage structures or tall stakes. The semi-determinate growth habit means Husky Red sets a substantial crop of fruits over a productive period and then slows significantly, unlike true indeterminate varieties that continue to grow and produce until frost. This concentrated production is an advantage for gardeners who want a substantial harvest over a defined period for preserving, canning, or fresh eating without managing continuous production throughout a long season. For container growing, Husky Red is one of the most practical full-sized slicing tomato varieties — its compact plant produces genuine slicing tomatoes (not just cherry or grape types) in a footprint manageable in a 15–20 gallon container. The VFNt resistance package provides meaningful protection against the soilborne diseases (Verticillium, Fusarium wilt, nematodes) and tobacco mosaic virus that limit tomato success in zone 9.
sources: Burpee Seeds — Husky Red tomato compact hybrid variety development and notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Semi-determinate tomato variety comparison for container and small-space growing
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tomato Variety Selection for Gulf South Home Gardens

common_name: Amelia Tomato (Disease-Resistant Hybrid Slicing Tomato with Broad Southeast-Adapted Resistance Package)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; Amelia is a vigorous indeterminate slicing tomato with a comprehensive VFFNTAs disease resistance package: Verticillium wilt (V), two races of Fusarium wilt (F, FF), nematodes (N), tobacco mosaic virus (T), and Alternaria stem canker (As). The Alternaria stem canker resistance in Amelia is a notable differentiator — Alternaria is a fungal pathogen (Alternaria alternata f. sp. lycopersici) that causes stem canker and early fruit death in susceptible varieties and is particularly prevalent in warm, humid conditions. Amelia was developed to address the full suite of southeastern disease pressures in a single variety, making it particularly appropriate for zone 9's warm, humid climate where multiple diseases commonly limit tomato production. Transplant March–April in zone 9. Fruits are medium-large (8–12 oz), round to slightly flat, and ripen to a deep, uniform red.

directions: Start seeds indoors; transplant after last frost and cage or stake to 6 feet. Harvest when uniformly deep red. Amelia Tomato is a commercially developed F1 hybrid slicing tomato specifically designed for production in the humid southeastern United States, where the combination of warm temperatures, high humidity, and disease inoculum in garden soils creates challenging conditions for standard tomato varieties. The variety's VFFNTAs resistance code represents one of the most comprehensive disease resistance packages available in a single tomato variety: resistance to Verticillium wilt (a soilborne fungal disease that colonizes the xylem and causes progressive wilting), two races of Fusarium wilt (a related soilborne pathogen with multiple strains that can overcome single-race resistance), root-knot nematodes (a serious pest in the warm, sandy soils of the Gulf Coast), tobacco mosaic virus (a seed- and contact-transmitted virus), and Alternaria stem canker (a foliar and stem fungal pathogen that can cause significant early-season damage in humid conditions). This combination of resistances directly addresses the primary disease challenges in zone 9 tomato production: nematode pressure is extremely common in the sandy soils of the Gulf Coast plain; Fusarium and Verticillium are endemic in soils with previous tomato production history; Alternaria stem canker increases in severity during warm, wet weather. For gardeners who have experienced repeated tomato crop failures in zone 9 from disease without knowing the specific cause, Amelia's broad resistance package provides protection across the most likely disease scenarios.
sources: Syngenta Vegetable Seeds — Amelia tomato F1 hybrid disease resistance documentation
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Disease-Resistant Tomato Variety Trials for Gulf South
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tomato Disease Management and Variety Selection

common_name: Isis Candy Cherry Tomato (Striped Sweet Cherry Tomato with Gold Cat-Eye Markings)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; Isis Candy is an indeterminate open-pollinated cherry tomato producing 1-inch round fruits that ripen to cherry-red with distinctive gold-yellow cat's-eye markings at the blossom end and subtle pink striping on the skin. In zone 9, transplant March–April and stake or cage; indeterminate types need support throughout the season. Isis Candy is an open-pollinated variety developed by tomato breeder Carolyn Male, one of the foremost American heirloom tomato breeders and authors of the landmark book '100 Heirloom Tomatoes for the American Garden.' The variety combines outstanding flavor — consistently rated among the sweetest and most complex of cherry tomatoes — with the striking visual appearance of the bicolor pattern. The gold-yellow markings develop clearly at full ripe (some cherry varieties show ambiguous color breaks; Isis Candy's blossom end marking is a clear, distinct gold circle on red). Production is prolific throughout zone 9's warm season.

directions: Start seeds indoors; transplant after last frost and cage or stake. Harvest when fully red with gold markings showing clearly at blossom end. Isis Candy is an open-pollinated cherry tomato variety developed by Carolyn Male, a New York tomato enthusiast and breeder who spent decades collecting and evaluating heirloom tomato varieties and documented her work in the widely respected '100 Heirloom Tomatoes for the American Garden.' Among cherry tomato varieties, Isis Candy stands out for both flavor quality and visual appeal: the flavor combines genuine tomato sweetness with a candy-like fruitiness and enough natural acidity to provide complexity rather than simple sweetness — the kind of balanced flavor profile that makes cherry tomatoes irresistible eaten directly off the vine. The appearance is striking: when ripe, the 1-inch round fruits display a candy-apple red with distinctive gold 'cat's-eye' markings — a golden-yellow circle centered at the blossom end — and sometimes faint pink striping on the red skin, creating a two-tone effect visible even from a distance in the garden. This visual distinction makes Isis Candy particularly attractive for multi-variety cherry tomato plantings where color diversity adds visual interest, and for farmers market and fresh-eating contexts where the unusual patterning captures attention. The variety is open-pollinated and stable, meaning saved seed reliably reproduces the variety's characteristics — an advantage over F1 hybrid cherry types that cannot be seed-saved. For zone 9 gardeners, Isis Candy's heat tolerance and prolific production throughout the Gulf Coast's long warm season make it a consistently productive choice among open-pollinated cherry varieties.
sources: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Isis Candy cherry tomato breeder documentation (Carolyn Male)
Seed Savers Exchange — Isis Candy open-pollinated cherry tomato variety notes
Carolyn Male — '100 Heirloom Tomatoes for the American Garden' (Workman Publishing)

common_name: Mariachi Pepper (AAS-Winning Mild Fruity Pepper with Multicolored Cluster Fruits)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Mariachi is a prolific, compact pepper plant that produces clusters of 3-inch elongated fruits in colors that transition through ivory, yellow, and orange to red as they mature — multiple colors appear simultaneously on the plant, creating a colorful and ornamental appearance. In zone 9, start seeds in January–February and transplant March–April. Mariachi won the All-America Selections award in 2006 for its outstanding productivity, compact plant habit, and unique combination of mild fruity flavor with very low heat (500–600 SHU, far below the heat level of jalapeño) that makes it versatile for both fresh eating and cooking without the heat restriction of spicier varieties. The individual fruits are tapered and elongated (3–4 inches) with a slightly irregular shape; the mild heat level means even heat-averse eaters can enjoy Mariachi fresh.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 75–85°F; transplant after last frost. Harvest at ivory-yellow for mild flavor or let ripen to red for maximum sweetness. Mariachi Pepper is a 2006 All-America Selections winner in the vegetable category, developed for its extraordinary visual display of multicolored fruits at different ripeness stages and its mildly spiced fruity flavor that makes it accessible to a wide range of palates. The name 'Mariachi' evokes the colorful, festive nature of the plant at peak production, when ivory, yellow, orange, and red fruits appear simultaneously in clusters on the same compact plant — the visual effect is immediately striking. Unlike standard bell pepper development where fruit transitions from green to fully ripe (green→red, green→yellow, etc.), Mariachi's fruit transition from ivory cream through progressive warm tones to red creates a more complex visual palette on the plant. The heat level of Mariachi is technically mild rather than sweet — at 500–600 SHU (Scoville Heat Units), it is substantially less hot than a jalapeño (2,500–8,000 SHU) but not as completely heat-free as a standard bell pepper. This small amount of heat adds a fruity warmth that enhances the flavor without causing heat discomfort for most people, making Mariachi useful in both sweet pepper contexts (fresh eating, salads) and mild chili contexts (salsas, roasting, stuffed preparations). The compact plant habit (24–30 inches) and prolific production of Mariachi's clusters make it an excellent candidate for container growing on patios and decks where the ornamental and culinary value combine effectively.
sources: All-America Selections — Mariachi pepper 2006 AAS winner documentation
PanAmerican Seed — Mariachi pepper variety development and production notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Pepper Variety Selection for Gulf South Home Gardens

common_name: Pineapple Orange (Classic Florida Sweet Orange with Aromatic Juice, Historical Commercial Variety)

planting.note: Plant grafted nursery trees in spring after cold risk has passed; Pineapple Orange is an important historical Florida sweet orange variety known for producing exceptionally sweet, aromatic juice with a distinctive pineapple-like fragrance when freshly squeezed. The pineapple aroma — from specific ester compounds in the juice — gives the variety its name and is most pronounced when the fruit is at peak ripeness (January–March in zone 9). Pineapple Orange is a mid-season variety, ripening between December and March in the Gulf South. Fruit is medium to large (3–3.5 inches diameter), slightly oblate, with a smooth, thin rind that becomes uniformly orange at maturity. The juice quality is among the best of the sweet orange varieties — high Brix (typically 11–13), excellent acid balance, and the distinctive pineapple-like aroma. Pineapple orange trees are moderately cold-sensitive (damaged at 27°F for extended periods) and best suited to zone 9a and warmer. They were once one of the most important commercial orange varieties in central Florida before the Navel orange and Valencia largely replaced them in commercial production.

directions: Plant grafted trees in well-drained soil with full sun. Water regularly in establishment year; tolerates some drought once established. Harvest January–March when rind is fully orange and fruit is fragrant. Pineapple Orange is one of the historically significant Florida orange varieties that shaped the state's commercial citrus industry in the late 19th and early 20th centuries — at its commercial peak, it was one of the leading sweet orange varieties grown in central Florida's ridge region, particularly in Polk and Orange Counties where the well-drained sandy loam soils and warm nights produced the highest-quality fruit. The name refers to the distinctive pineapple-like aroma of freshly squeezed Pineapple Orange juice — a volatile ester profile distinct from other orange varieties that creates a complex aromatic quality prized by juice connoisseurs. Florida horticulturists identified the variety in the 1860s–1870s as particularly valuable for its juice quality, and it became an important commercial variety until it was gradually displaced by Navel oranges (which offer better fresh-eating convenience with their seedless character) and Valencia oranges (which hold on the tree longer for extended harvest seasons). Despite its loss of commercial dominance, Pineapple Orange remained a beloved home garden and specialty variety in Florida, maintained by homeowners and small growers who valued its juice quality over the commercial virtues (navel convenience, Valencia storage) that made other varieties dominant. For zone 9a Gulf Coast gardeners, Pineapple Orange remains one of the most rewarding sweet orange varieties for home juice production — the fruit sets reliably in zone 9's warm winters, ripens to exceptional sweetness and flavor in January–March when fresh citrus is most welcome, and the tree's moderate size makes it manageable in home landscapes.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Pineapple sweet orange historical Florida variety documentation
Florida Department of Citrus — Historical Florida citrus variety production and Pineapple orange
Citrus Industry Magazine — Heritage Florida citrus varieties and Pineapple orange juice quality
